target.title: fix(cron): hint after disable about list filtering disabled jobs
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw cron disable <id>` appears to delete recurring cron jobs because `openclaw cron list` hides disabled jobs by default. Users see a successful disable response, then `cron list` shows the job is gone, and reasonably conclude the job was deleted.
- Why it matters: UX confusion can lead to users recreating cron jobs from scratch (losing custom messages, models, delivery targets) when their original disabled job is intact and one `cron enable` away.
- What changed: Added a one-line stderr hint after `cron disable` pointing the user at `cron list --all` and `cron enable <id>`. Plus a regression test that locks in the round-trip behavior.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No semantic changes to `cron disable`, `cron list`, `cron list` defaults, the gateway handler, the storage layer, or any other cron command. The only new behavior is one stderr line.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #(none — opened directly per CONTRIBUTING.md "Bugs & small fixes → Open a PR")
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `src/cron/service/ops.ts:230` filters disabled jobs from `list()` unless `includeDisabled: true` is passed; the CLI's `cron list` only sets `includeDisabled` when invoked with `--all`. The disable command path is correct; the surprise is that the default `cron list` doesn't show the result of a successful disable.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No regression test covered the round-trip "disable → list (default) → list (--all) → enable" flow on a recurring job. This PR adds it.
- Contributing context: Reproduces on 2026.3.13 and 2026.4.29.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/cron/service/ops.update.disable-and-list.test.ts` (new)
- Scenario: create recurring `{kind: "every"}` job → disable → assert `list()` (default) does NOT include it → assert `list({includeDisabled: true})` DOES include it with `enabled: false` and the original schedule/payload preserved → assert `enable()` makes it visible again.
- Why smallest reliable guardrail: pure ops-layer test, uses existing `setupCronRegressionFixtures` helper, no I/O beyond a temp store path.
- Existing test that already covers this: none found.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

One stderr line after `openclaw cron disable <id>`:

```
Note: 'openclaw cron list' hides disabled jobs by default. Use 'openclaw cron list --all' to see this job, or 'openclaw cron enable <id>' to re-enable it.
```

No change to stdout (the CLI still prints the job JSON). No change to cron behavior, list defaults, or any other surface.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[user] openclaw cron disable <id>
   -> CLI prints job JSON with enabled:false
[user] openclaw cron list
   -> job not shown
[user concludes "deleted"; recreates job from scratch]

After:
[user] openclaw cron disable <id>
   -> CLI prints job JSON with enabled:false
   -> CLI prints stderr hint about --all and `cron enable`
[user] openclaw cron list --all
   -> job visible with enabled:false
[user] openclaw cron enable <id>
   -> job re-enabled, original definition intact
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No (only adds a stderr line to existing command)
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.3.1 (arm64)
- Runtime/container: Node v23.11.0, pnpm 10.33.2
- Model/provider: N/A (CLI-only change)
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config: defaults

### Steps (live local against running gateway pid 25457, port 18789)

1. `node openclaw.mjs cron add --name "claude-fork-smoke-..." --cron "0 4 * * *" --message "smoke" --no-deliver`
2. Note jobId from output.
3. `node openclaw.mjs cron disable <jobId>` — observe the new stderr hint.
4. `node openclaw.mjs cron disable <jobId> 2>/dev/null` — confirm hint absent on stdout.
5. `node openclaw.mjs cron enable <jobId>` — confirm no hint.
6. `node openclaw.mjs cron list` — disabled job hidden.
7. `node openclaw.mjs cron list --all` — disabled job visible with `disabled` status.
8. `node openclaw.mjs cron rm <jobId>` — cleanup.

### Expected

Stderr hint appears after step 3. Hint absent in step 4 (pipe-safe). No hint in step 5. Round-trip in steps 6–7 matches expected list-filter semantics.

### Actual

(After fix) Matches expected, verified end-to-end against the running gateway. (Before fix) Step 3 prints job JSON only; step 7 with `--all` works, but without `--all` the job is invisible — leading users to think it was deleted.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — see `src/cron/service/ops.update.disable-and-list.test.ts`. Targeted run: `pnpm test src/cron/service/ops.update.disable-and-list.test.ts` → 1 passed (1) in 23ms. Full changed-test gate: `pnpm test:changed` → 4 Vitest shards passed in 13.90s.
- [x] Full `pnpm test` ran on both this branch and clean upstream `main` (via `git worktree`). Both runs ended with `[test] failed 77 Vitest shards` and **the failure sets are identical** — same 14 failing test cases across `runtime-config`, `secrets`, and `plugins` lanes (`config.pruning-defaults.test.ts`, `model-alias-defaults.test.ts`, `schema.base.generated.test.ts`, `runtime.coverage.test.ts`, `target-registry.docs.test.ts`, `bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts`, `plugin-registry-snapshot.test.ts`). `diff` of the sorted FAIL lines between the two logs is empty. Conclusion: these failures are pre-existing on `main`, environmental (Mac-local), and unrelated to this diff. None of the failing lanes overlap with the cron/CLI surfaces this PR touches.
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording — captured terminal output below from live local run against the running gateway pid 25457 on a clean environment. (Image screenshots available on request; textual capture is sufficient for a CLI-stderr change.)

```
=== Step 2: cron disable — combined stdout + stderr (hint visible at end of stderr) ===

$ node openclaw.mjs cron disable <id>
{
  "id": "f83349c2-bb2b-4686-ba88-6521f29fdd4d",
  "name": "claude-fork-smoke-clean-1778021015",
  "enabled": false,
  ...
}
Note: 'openclaw cron list' hides disabled jobs by default. Use 'openclaw cron list --all' to see this job, or 'openclaw cron enable <id>' to re-enable it.

=== Step 3: cron disable, stdout-only (2>/dev/null) — hint absent, JSON pipelines unaffected ===

$ node openclaw.mjs cron disable <id> 2>/dev/null
{
  "id": "f83349c2-bb2b-4686-ba88-6521f29fdd4d",
  "name": "claude-fork-smoke-clean-1778021015",
  "enabled": false,
  ...
}

=== Step 4: cron enable — hint NOT fired (params.enabled === true guard) ===

$ node openclaw.mjs cron enable <id> 2>&1
{
  "id": "f83349c2-bb2b-4686-ba88-6521f29fdd4d",
  "name": "claude-fork-smoke-clean-1778021015",
  "enabled": true,
  ...
}

=== Step 5: round-trip — default list hides the disabled job; list --all shows it ===

$ node openclaw.mjs cron list                 # smoke job NOT shown (default filter)
$ node openclaw.mjs cron list --all           # smoke job shown with status=disabled
```

- [ ] Perf numbers — N/A

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw cron disable <id>` makes recurring cron jobs appear deleted, because the default `openclaw cron list` filters out disabled jobs. After this fix, the CLI prints a one-line stderr hint after a successful disable, pointing the user at `openclaw cron list --all` and `openclaw cron enable <id>`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 26.3.1 (arm64), Node v23.11.0, pnpm 10.33.2. Live local OpenClaw gateway pid 25457 listening on `ws://127.0.0.1:18789`, gateway version OpenClaw 2026.5.5, running the fork's compiled CLI (`node openclaw.mjs ...` from [openclaw-fork/](https://github.com/kate/openclaw-fork) at commit `ddf0858f6f`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `node openclaw.mjs cron add --name "claude-fork-smoke-clean-1778021015" --cron "0 4 * * *" --message "smoke" --no-deliver` → captured `id=f83349c2-bb2b-4686-ba88-6521f29fdd4d`
  2. `node openclaw.mjs cron disable <id>` (combined stdout+stderr)
  3. `node openclaw.mjs cron disable <id> 2>/dev/null` (stdout-only, to verify pipe-safety)
  4. `node openclaw.mjs cron enable <id>` (verify hint does NOT fire on enable)
  5. `node openclaw.mjs cron disable <id>` then `node openclaw.mjs cron list` (verify disabled job hidden by default)
  6. `node openclaw.mjs cron list --all` (verify disabled job visible with `disabled` status)
  7. `node openclaw.mjs cron rm <id>` (cleanup)
- **Evidence after fix:** Live terminal output captured from running the fork's CLI against the local gateway:

  ```
  $ node openclaw.mjs cron disable f83349c2-bb2b-4686-ba88-6521f29fdd4d
  {
    "id": "f83349c2-bb2b-4686-ba88-6521f29fdd4d",
    "name": "claude-fork-smoke-clean-1778021015",
    "enabled": false,
    "schedule": { "kind": "cron", "expr": "0 4 * * *" },
    ...
  }
  Note: 'openclaw cron list' hides disabled jobs by default. Use 'openclaw cron list --all' to see this job, or 'openclaw cron enable <id>' to re-enable it.

  $ node openclaw.mjs cron disable <id> 2>/dev/null
  { "enabled": false, ... }   # clean JSON, no Note line on stdout

  $ node openclaw.mjs cron enable <id> 2>&1
  { "enabled": true, ... }    # no Note line — guard correctly skips on enable

  $ node openclaw.mjs cron list --all
  ID                                   Name                              Schedule                Status
  f83349c2-bb2b-4686-ba88-6521f29fdd4d claude-fork-smoke-clean-...       cron 0 4 * * *          disabled

  $ node openclaw.mjs cron rm <id>
  { "ok": true, "removed": true }
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The new stderr hint appeared exactly once on stderr immediately after the disable JSON output. The hint did NOT appear on `cron enable`, confirming the `params.enabled === false` guard. Stdout-only consumers (`2>/dev/null`) saw clean JSON with no contamination — JSON pipelines are unaffected. After re-disabling, default `cron list` correctly hid the job; `cron list --all` showed it with status `disabled`. Cleanup via `cron rm` removed the smoke job (`{ "ok": true, "removed": true }`).
- **What was not tested:** Control UI behavior — this PR is CLI-only and does not change the Control UI. Non-`cron` channel routes were not exercised because the change is gated to the cron disable command path.

## AI-Assisted Disclosure

- [x] Marked as AI-assisted (this PR uses Claude Code)
- [x] Degree of testing: lightly tested — local `pnpm build && pnpm check` green; targeted `pnpm test:changed` green (4 shards, includes cron + cli lanes); live local CLI smoke against running gateway verifies stderr hint appears on disable, absent on enable, absent on stdout pipe.
- [ ] Prompts / session logs: optional — happy to attach as a comment if reviewers request.
- [x] I have read and understand every line of this change.
- [ ] Codex review: deferring to the GitHub Codex review bot when this PR opens. Will run `codex review --base origin/main` locally if the bot does not fire within ~24h (CONTRIBUTING.md:137).
- [x] I will resolve or reply to bot review conversations after I address them.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: stderr hint appears after `cron disable`; `cron list --all` shows the disabled job; `cron enable` restores it without printing the hint. Pipe-safety: stdout-only consumers (`2>/dev/null`) see clean JSON with no hint contamination. New regression test passes against the fix.
- Edge cases checked: `enable` (params.enabled === true) does NOT print the hint; piping disable output to `jq`-style stdout consumers is unaffected.
- What I did NOT verify: behavior in the Control UI (no UI change in this PR; the hint is CLI-only).
- Full `pnpm test` was run on both this branch and a clean `main` worktree; both produced identical failure sets (14 failures across `runtime-config`/`secrets`/`plugins` lanes). The failures are pre-existing on `main` and environmental to my Mac. Confirmed via `diff` of sorted `FAIL` lines between the two logs (empty diff). Targeted `pnpm test:changed` on this branch covers cron/cli/unit lanes — all green.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I will reply to or resolve every bot review conversation I address.
- [x] I will leave unresolved only conversations that need reviewer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes (additive stderr line; no contract change)
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: stderr noise on stdout-redirecting consumers (`openclaw cron disable | jq ...`).
  - Mitigation: hint is on stderr, not stdout. Verified live: `cron disable <id> 2>/dev/null` produces clean JSON with no hint.
- Risk: `params.enabled === false` guard doesn't cover the `disable` path if internals change.
  - Mitigation: `params` is the registration-time config object captured in the closure of `registerCronToggleCommand` (see `src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-simple.ts:54-58`); structurally stable. The new test asserts both enable-no-hint and disable-with-hint paths via the underlying ops layer.

---

target.title: fix(control-ui): don't hard-deny local attachments before bootstrap roots load
target.body:
## Summary
- Skip the client-side `localMediaPreviewRoots` gate while roots are still empty (bootstrap config not yet loaded), so local `MEDIA:` attachments fall through to the authoritative `assistant-media?meta=1` server check instead of being permanently shown as \"Outside allowed folders\". Once roots are populated, behavior is unchanged.
- Mirrors the same fix to `resolveRenderableMessageImages` so local images aren't filtered out during the same bootstrap window.
- Adds a regression test in `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts` and a CHANGELOG entry.

Closes #67915

## Testing
- `pnpm vitest run ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts` (36/36, includes 1 new)
- Reverting just the source change makes the new test fail with \"Outside allowed folders\", confirming it locks the behaviour in.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Issue #67915 reports local TTS audio rendering as \"Unavailable — Outside allowed folders\" in Control UI even when the server confirms `{\"available\": true}`. Cause is the client-side gate running before bootstrap roots populate. Patch falls through to the server check during that window.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout from this PR branch on macOS (Darwin 25.5.0) at HEAD de7a08d8b88494d219ed2361a01ef0c877ac4e1b. Vitest runs from the branch worktree against the changed UI module.

- What was not tested: A live OpenClaw runtime on Linux/OpenShift was not exercised; the fix is UI-side and the regression test reproduces the exact gating bug.

target.title: fix(feishu): serialize OpenClaw messages into Feishu v3 send payload
target.body:
## Summary
- Add a source-level Feishu serializer for OpenClaw outbound envelopes that contain `payloads`, `to`, and `channel`.
- Route Feishu `send` message actions with `payloads` through the serializer before calling the Feishu create-message API.
- Add mocked Feishu API regression coverage for target inference, Chinese text, internal-field stripping, content stringification, and interactive card content.

## Root cause
Some OpenClaw delivery paths can hand Feishu an internal outbound envelope shape instead of a Feishu API v3 create-message payload. That leaks internal-only fields like `payloads`, `channel`, `replyToTag`, and `audioAsVoice`, and omits required Feishu fields.

## Payload before/after
Before:
```json
{
  "payloads": [{ "text": "hello", "replyToTag": false, "audioAsVoice": false }],
```

```json
{

}
```

target.title: cron: reject webhook URLs with embedded credentials
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Cron webhook URLs could include embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:pass@example.com/hook), which can leak secrets in logs, config, or URLs. - Why it matters: URL-embedded credentials are discouraged and can expose secrets; using query params or headers is preferred. - What changed: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl now rejects URLs with username or password in the parsed URL. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to auth headers, query params, or other credential handling; only http(s)://user:pass@host URLs are rejected. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Cron webhook URLs with embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:secret@host/path) are now rejected as invalid. URLs without credentials continue to work. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin) - Runtime/container: Node 22+ - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): CRON webhook delivery - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Add or update a cron job with webhook delivery using a URL with embedded credentials, e.g. https://user:pass@example.invalid/webhook. 2. Observe that the webhook URL is rejected (job will not use it). 3. Use the same job with https://example.invalid/webhook (no credentials) and confirm it is accepted. ### Expected - URLs with user:pass@ in the authority are rejected. - URLs without embedded credentials continue to work. ### Actual - Behavior matches above ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl returns null for URLs like http://user:pass@host/path; returns the URL for http://host/path. - Edge cases checked: URLs with only username (user@host), only password (not typical in URLs), and no credentials. - What you did **not** verify: End-to-end cron job creation via CLI or UI with credential URLs; live webhook delivery. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`No`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`Yes`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: Update any cron job webhook URLs that include user:pass@ in the URL. Remove embedded credentials and use headers or query params for auth instead. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert this commit. - Files/config to restore: src/cron/webhook-url.ts - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Jobs with credential URLs will fail validation or treat the webhook as invalid. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. target.title: [codex] Add current OpenRouter image models
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Related #91504.

OpenRouter image generation currently exposes only a small static shortcut list, so users asking for broader OpenRouter image model support do not see current image-output models in OpenClaw's provider metadata or generated model catalog. Current OpenRouter image models are also documented under OpenRouter's dedicated Images API, so exposing them through the old chat-completions path would advertise models that can fail at runtime.

## Why This Change Was Made

OpenRouter's public `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/images/models` response currently advertises additional image models beyond the existing OpenClaw list, including:

- `google/gemini-3.1-flash-image`
- `google/gemini-3-pro-image`
- `google/gemini-2.5-flash-image`
- `openai/gpt-5-image`
- `openai/gpt-5-image-mini`
- `microsoft/mai-image-2.5`

This PR adds those current model ids to the curated OpenRouter image-generation provider list, routes them through OpenRouter's dedicated `/images` endpoint, and parses the Images API `data[].b64_json` response shape. Existing preview/default chat-image model refs remain accepted on the chat-completions path so already configured users are not migrated unexpectedly.

OpenRouter `/images` supported parameters are model-specific, so this PR also gates the new models before submission:

- Gemini and Microsoft `/images` models request one output at a time.
- Microsoft receives only supported aspect-ratio/reference-image fields and rejects unsupported resolution requests clearly.
- OpenAI image models reject explicit unsupported Gemini-style `aspectRatio`/`resolution` requests instead of silently dropping them.
- Tool-inferred edit resolution is marked as inferred so OpenRouter can ignore unsupported inferred hints without breaking OpenAI/Microsoft reference-image edits.
- Legacy/default chat-image models keep their previous 5-reference-image guard while current `/images` models can use the shared tool cap of 10 when supported.

This intentionally remains a curated-list update, not dynamic catalog discovery; the broader catalog policy can still be considered separately.

## User Impact

Users can now discover and configure more current OpenRouter image models for the `image_generate` tool without relying on undocumented override strings. The linked issue's `microsoft/mai-image-2.5` example is included. Unsupported model-specific count, geometry, or reference-image requests fail locally with clear errors instead of being sent to OpenRouter and rejected later.

Existing OpenRouter image configs continue to work because the previous preview ids remain in the list and the default is unchanged.

## Evidence

- OpenRouter docs: current image generation uses `POST /api/v1/images` and returns image data through `data[].b64_json`: https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/overview/multimodal/image-generation
- Queried OpenRouter's live `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/images/models` endpoint on 2026-06-28 and confirmed the six added ids are present. Live capability proof showed model-specific `n`, `aspect_ratio`, `resolution`, and `input_references` support, including Microsoft `n.max=1`, Microsoft no `resolution`, OpenAI no `aspect_ratio`/`resolution`, and Gemini/Microsoft/OpenAI different reference limits.
- Built the local CLI runtime with bundled Node: `/Users/AdityaWork/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin/node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`.
- Runtime provider-list proof: `/Users/AdityaWork/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin/node openclaw.mjs capability image providers --json | jq '.[] | select(.id == "openrouter") | {id, defaultModel, models, generate: .capabilities.generate, edit: .capabilities.edit, geometry: .capabilities.geometry}'` showed OpenRouter exposing all six added ids, `defaultModel: google/gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview`, `generate.maxCount: 4`, `edit.maxInputImages: 10`, aspect ratios including `1:4`, `1:8`, `4:1`, `8:1`, and resolutions `1K`, `2K`, `4K`.
- Redacted no-paid runtime generation proof using the compiled OpenRouter provider and a local HTTP fixture with explicit test SSRF allowance: `provider.generateImage({ model: "google/gemini-3.1-flash-image", count: 1, aspectRatio: "1:4", resolution: "1K" })` posted to `/api/v1/images` with authorization redacted, body `{ model: "google/gemini-3.1-flash-image", n: 1, aspect_ratio: "1:4", resolution: "1K" }`, parsed an Images API-style `{ data: [{ b64_json, media_type: "image/png" }] }` response, and returned one `image/png` asset (`image-1.png`, 68 bytes). This exercises the compiled provider endpoint/body/response path without making a paid live OpenRouter generation request.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openrouter/image-generation-provider.test.ts src/image-generation/runtime.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts`
- `node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/openrouter/image-generation-provider.ts extensions/openrouter/image-generation-provider.test.ts docs/providers/openrouter.md docs/tools/image-generation.md src/image-generation/types.ts src/image-generation/runtime-types.ts src/image-generation/runtime.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm docs:list`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --codex-bin /tmp/codex-fast-wrapper` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings after per-model caps, docs alignment, runtime `resolutionInferred` plumbing, and legacy chat-image reference guard.

target.title: fix(discord): disable ws 8.21.0 receiver part limits for gateway sockets
target.body:
## Summary

ws 8.21.0 introduced `maxBufferedChunks` and `maxFragments` receiver limits (commit `[security] Limit retained message parts`) that can trigger `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and close connections with code 1008 under certain traffic patterns.

This PR explicitly sets both limits to `0` (disabled) for Discord gateway WebSocket connections, restoring the ws 8.20.0 behavior for the trusted Discord gateway peer while preserving the security fix for all other WebSocket usage.

## Changes

- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.ts`**: Base class `createWebSocket()` now passes `maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0` with a `ws.ClientOptions` cast (since `@types/ws` 8.18.1 does not yet expose these fields).
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.ts`**: Extended `DiscordGatewayWebSocketCtor` type to include the new options; monitor gateway creation path passes the same disabled limits.
- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.test.ts`**: Added test verifying base class WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.test.ts`**: Added test verifying monitor plugin WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.proxy.test.ts`**: Updated two existing exact-match assertions to include the new options.

## Why `0` instead of raising the limits

- ws 8.21.0 treats `0` as disabled (`receiver.js:61`, `receiver.js:98`: `if (this._maxBufferedChunks > 0 && ...)`), matching the pre-8.21.0 behavior exactly.
- Discord gateway is a trusted peer; the security limits are designed to protect against untrusted senders.
- Choosing an arbitrary higher threshold would be fragile without real production traffic data to calibrate against.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: ws 8.21.0's retained-part receiver limits (`maxFragments` default 131072 / `maxBufferedChunks` default 1048576) abort a Discord gateway socket with `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` / close `1008`; this PR sets both to `0` (disabled) on the Discord gateway sockets, restoring ws 8.20.0 behavior for that trusted peer.
- **Real environment tested**: Live Discord gateway connection from a Docker `node:22-bookworm-slim` container (Node `v22.22.3`) with the repo-pinned `ws@8.21.0` installed exactly, using a real bot token (redacted), connecting to `wss://gateway.discord.gg/?v=10&encoding=json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: build a `node:22-bookworm-slim` image with `npm install ws@8.21.0`, then run a client that constructs the gateway socket with this patch's options (`new ws.WebSocket(url, { handshakeTimeout: 30000, maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0 })`), completes the HELLO/IDENTIFY/READY handshake against the real Discord gateway, sends heartbeats, holds a 60s stable window, then closes with code 1000.
- **Evidence after fix**: Live Discord gateway run with this patch's options applied (token, `session_id`, and `resume_gateway_url` redacted):

```
[env] node=v22.22.3 ws=8.21.0
[discord] connecting wss://gateway.discord.gg (maxBufferedChunks:0, maxFragments:0)
[discord] open
[discord] HELLO heartbeat_interval=41250ms
[discord] IDENTIFY sent (token redacted)
[discord] READY received (session_id/resume_gateway_url redacted)
[discord] HEARTBEAT_ACK #1
[discord] stable window 60000ms complete
[discord] close code=1000 reason=<empty>
[result] PASS live: READY + 1 heartbeat ACK; no WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS during stable window
```

Supplemental deterministic mechanism repro against a local ws 8.21.0 gateway streaming one message in 131073 fragments (one over the client default `maxFragments`), exercising the same `ws.WebSocket` receiver path:

```
# default ws.WebSocket (ws 8.21 defaults)
[before] open
[before] error code=WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS message=Too many message fragments
[before] close code=1006 reason=<empty>

# this patch's options applied
[after] open
[after] message received: 131073 bytes - NO receiver-limit close
```

- **Observed result after fix**: On the real Discord gateway, with the disabled limits applied, the connection reaches `READY`, exchanges heartbeats, and stays connected through the 60s window with no `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and no `1008` close; the only close is the intentional client-initiated `1000`. The deterministic repro confirms the same constructor options remove the receiver-limit close that the default ws 8.21 client hits.
- **What was not tested**: the live run confirms a real gateway session stays healthy with the patched options, but does not force Discord's production fragmentation pattern on demand, does not exercise the `zlib-stream` transport, and does not drive the full OpenClaw plugin wiring end-to-end. The deterministic repro proves the ws 8.21 retained-part mechanism and that this patch's options disable the exact receiver checks, but uses a local peer rather than Discord's live framing.

## Tests

- `gateway.test.ts`: 25 passed
- `gateway-plugin.test.ts`: 17 passed  
- `provider.proxy.test.ts`: 21 passed
- TypeScript check (tsgo): clean

Fixes #88330

target.body: ## Summary Telegram native slash-command menus are now synced and cleared for default, private-chat, and group-chat scopes. This prevents private chats from retaining stale command menus when the bot updates or disables native commands. AI-assisted by Codex GPT-5.5. ## What Changed - Added `all_private_chats` to the shared Telegram command-menu scope list. - Added private-chat cleanup to the explicit native-disabled command cleanup path. - Kept command construction, authorization, transport behavior, and config unchanged. - Kept unchanged-menu skip logging on the verbose-only path, matching the pre-existing rate-limit guard behavior. - Added focused regression coverage for scope ordering, empty-menu cleanup, cache skips, localized command variants, retry behavior, native-disabled cleanup, and unchanged-menu logging. ## Scope Boundaries - No new permissions or secrets. - No dependency, package, or CI changes. - No change to the commands themselves; only the Telegram Bot API scopes used to set and clear native command menus changed. ## Verification Verified on rebased head `6798383d1ae9bace7d6cce5a4552f47b13401e58` against base `84b025eb622176483ebb1615a88059a4368b3775`. - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-command-menu.test.ts` passed: 1 file, 26 tests. - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-command-menu.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts` passed: 2 files, 51 tests. - `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-command-menu.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-command-menu.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts` passed. - `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` produced no output. ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram native command menu sync now deletes and sets commands for `default`, `all_private_chats`, and `all_group_chats`; empty-menu cleanup also clears the private-chat scope. - **Real environment tested:** Local Node 24 runtime against current PR head `6798383d1ae9bace7d6cce5a4552f47b13401e58`, executing the production `syncTelegramMenuCommands` path with a Bot API capture adapter for `deleteMyCommands` and `setMyCommands` calls. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** From the PR worktree, ran a `pnpm exec tsx` probe that imported `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-command-menu.ts`, invoked `syncTelegramMenuCommands` once with a non-empty command list and once with an empty command list, and captured the emitted Telegram Bot API method/options sequence. - **Evidence after fix:** Current-head terminal output from the Bot API call-sequence probe: ```json { "head": "6798383d1ae9bace7d6cce5a4552f47b13401e58", "base": "84b025eb622176483ebb1615a88059a4368b3775", "registrationCalls": [ { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "default" }, { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "all_private_chats" }, { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "all_group_chats" }, { "method": "setMyCommands", "scope": "default", "commandCount": 1, "languageCode": null }, { "method": "setMyCommands", "scope": "all_private_chats", "commandCount": 1, "languageCode": null }, { "method": "setMyCommands", "scope": "all_group_chats", "commandCount": 1, "languageCode": null } ], "cleanupCalls": [ { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "default" }, { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "all_private_chats" }, { "method": "deleteMyCommands", "scope": "all_group_chats" } ], "runtimeLogs": [], "runtimeErrors": [] } ``` The proof from my telegram client: - **Observed result after fix:** The production sync path emitted delete-then-set calls for default, private-chat, and group-chat scopes, and the empty-menu path emitted deletes for all three scopes without setting any commands. - **What was not tested:** I did not run a live Telegram Bot API token or Telegram Desktop visual proof in this workspace. That still needs Mantis/maintainer proof if the project requires remote Telegram UI confirmation. ## Review Status - No inline review threads remain open. - The latest ClawSweeper review finding about unchanged-menu skip logging was addressed in `6798383d1ae9bace7d6cce5a4552f47b13401e58`. - ClawSweeper may still ask for live Telegram proof or an explicit maintainer proof override before merge.

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Preserve `fs.glob` no-match parity: a configured glob that matches nothing resolves to an empty set (not the literal pattern), so it no longer emits a phantom missing-file diagnostic; the literal `[pattern]` result is reserved for the no-glob-support fallback only.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`** / **`src/hooks/internal-hook-dispatch.ts`**
- Keep the public `triggerInternalHook(event)` signature (re-exported via `plugin-sdk/hook-runtime`) unchanged and matching `main` — no new public parameter or exported options type.
- Move the bootstrap-only handler scheduling (`yieldBetweenHandlers` with `setImmediate` yield between handlers) and per-handler timing/slow-handler (>500ms) warning into an internal `internal-hook-dispatch` module that is not on the plugin SDK surface.

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable yielding between handlers for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers (via the internal dispatch entrypoint)

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Keep the public `resolveBootstrapFilesForRun` params (re-exported via `plugin-sdk/agent-harness-runtime`) callback-free and matching `main`; route embedded-runner substage timing through an internal `resolveBootstrapFilesForRunWithTiming` variant kept off the SDK surface.
- Support the new async workspace file loading interface.

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning exercised through the internal dispatch entrypoint
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — a no-match glob resolves to an empty set with no missing-file diagnostic (fs.glob parity)

The public plugin SDK surface is unchanged: `node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs` exits 0 with no new package-exported entrypoints. All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

target.title: fix(google): bound embedding-batch JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where three success-path JSON reads in `extensions/google/embedding-batch.ts` are unbounded, allowing a misbehaving or hostile Gemini endpoint to stream an arbitrarily large response body and cause memory pressure or OOM.

The three affected surfaces are:

1. **`submitGeminiBatch` — file-upload response** (`/upload/v1beta/files?uploadType=multipart`): returns `{ name?, file? }` identifying the uploaded batch file. Error path uses `fileRes.text()` (bounded via `readResponseTextLimited` elsewhere in the project), success path had no cap.

2. **`submitGeminiBatch` — batch-create response** (`:asyncBatchEmbedContent`): returns the initial `GeminiBatchStatus` after batch submission. Error path uses `batchRes.text()` (bounded), success path had no cap.

3. **`fetchGeminiBatchStatus` — batch-status poll response**: called in a polling loop to track batch progress. Error path already used `createProviderHttpError` (bounded), success path had no cap. This is the hottest path of the three — it is called once per poll interval for the lifetime of every running batch.

This asymmetric pattern (error bounded, success unbounded) is the same gap fixed for sibling surfaces in #95418, #95420 (OpenRouter model catalog), #96027 (Ollama discovery), and #96868 (OpenAI-compatible embeddings).

The Gemini client accepts a configurable `baseUrl` — when an operator or memory plugin is misconfigured against a hostile endpoint, all three paths are reachable with no cap.

## Why This Change Was Made

`readProviderJsonResponse` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` applies the standard 16 MiB cap, cancels the `ReadableStream` on overflow, and surfaces `"<label>: JSON response exceeds N bytes"` — a single-call replacement for each bare `await response.json()`. Net change is +1 LOC on the import and 0 on the production logic per call site.

## User Impact

No change for well-behaved Gemini endpoints. Memory embedding batch jobs running against a self-hosted, misconfigured, or compromised `baseUrl` that returns an oversized success payload will now see a bounded error and early stream cancellation instead of unbounded heap growth.

## Evidence

**Proof script** (`node scripts/proof-google-embedding-batch-bound.mjs`, Node v22.22.0):

```text
[case 1] gemini.batch-file-upload oversized, ReadableStream, cap=1 MiB
  ok: rejected on oversized file-upload body — true
  ok: bounded error message present (got: Content too large: 2097152 bytes (limit: 1048576 bytes)) — true
  ok: stream cancelled before all 20 chunks (readCount=2) — true
  ok: cancel() was called — true

[case 2] gemini.batch-create oversized, ReadableStream, cap=1 MiB
  ok: rejected on oversized batch-create body — true
  ok: stream cancelled — true
  ok: readCount < 20 — true

[case 3] gemini.batch-status oversized, node:http, bytes-on-wire
  ok: rejected on oversized batch-status body — true
  ok: bounded error message present (got: Content too large: 1071896 bytes (limit: 1048576 bytes)) — true

[case 4] negative control — raw response.json() does NOT cancel
  ok: unbounded .json() drained all 20 chunks (readCount=20) — true
  ok: stream NOT cancelled — drained to EOF — true

[case 5] small upload/batch/status bodies parse cleanly
  ok: file-upload small body parsed — true
  ok: batch-create/status small body parsed — true

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

**Local test suite** (`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/google/embedding-batch.test.ts`, Vitest v4.1.8, Node v22.22.0):

```text
 ✓ bounds oversized file-upload JSON response and cancels the stream
 ✓ bounds oversized batch-create JSON response and cancels the stream
 ✓ bounds oversized batch-status poll JSON response and cancels the stream
 ✓ parses small responses on all three JSON paths correctly

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
   Duration  2.07s
```

What was not tested: live Gemini API calls.

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix: preserve plugin hook and tool closure state
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes plugin tool resolution so cached descriptor wrappers do not bypass an already-live plugin registry that owns both hooks and tools for the same plugin. That keeps hook handlers and tool factories on the same `register()` closure when a plugin uses closure state across `before_prompt_build` and a tool like `memory_add`.

## Changes

- Detect active/pinned-channel registries that already contain a plugin's tools and hooks.
- Skip cached descriptor wrappers for those stateful hook+tool plugins, forcing the normal live-registry materialization path.
- Preserve descriptor caching for plain tool-only plugins and cold/no-live-registry paths.
- Add regression coverage for a warmed descriptor cache being bypassed when a live hook+tool registry is available.

## Testing

- `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-shim:$PATH" pnpm vitest run src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` — passed, 59 tests.
- `git diff --check` — passed.
- `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-shim:$PATH" node scripts/check-changed.mjs` — all gates passed through final pairing-account guard; process was SIGKILLed at final exit on this host. Standalone `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-shim:$PATH" node scripts/check-pairing-account-scope.mjs` passed.

Fixes #77822

target.title: fix(gateway): disable startup trace monitor on startup failure
target.body:
## Summary
- add an explicit `startupTrace.close()` cleanup path in gateway startup tracing
- call `startupTrace.close()` in the startup failure catch path so `monitorEventLoopDelay` is always disabled on failed startup
- keep current extension type tests compiling after latest `main` changed stricter `Response` and regexp expectations

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway startup tracing with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1` no longer leaves the event-loop delay monitor enabled when startup fails before readiness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout of this PR branch at `e81371fe6a0bbc8a84d5c1a51a7a821611f268fa`, running Node through the repo's `tsx` loader against a real temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Created a temporary invalid `openclaw.json`, then ran:
  ```sh
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmpdir/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1 timeout 20s node --import tsx -e 'import("./src/gateway/server.impl.ts").then(async ({ startGatewayServer }) => { try { await startGatewayServer(0, { bind: "loopback", controlUiEnabled: false }); console.log("unexpected startup success"); process.exitCode = 2; } catch (error) { console.log("startup failed as expected"); console.log(String(error instanceof Error ? error.message : error).split("\n")[0]); } })'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the after-fix run:
  ```text
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.file 0.5ms total=26.7ms eventLoopMax=9.9ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.hash 0.2ms total=47.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.parse 0.3ms total=49.3ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read 28.1ms total=53.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot 30.6ms total=55.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  startup failed as expected
  Invalid config at /tmp/openclaw-proof-4texI2/openclaw.json.
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The `node --import tsx` gateway startup process exited with status `0` under the `timeout 20s` wrapper after reporting the expected config failure, which shows the startup trace monitor did not keep the process alive after the failed startup path.
- **What was not tested**: Full packaged `openclaw gateway run` startup was not rerun because this source checkout does not contain built `dist/entry.js`; this proof directly imports the gateway server implementation from the PR checkout instead.

## Verification

target.title: feat(plugins): add Control UI entry points
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Plugins can expose gateway-authenticated HTTP routes, but there was no generic way for a plugin to register a first-class Control UI navigation entry.
- What changed: Added generic Plugin UI Entry Points across the plugin registry, gateway protocol, launch-token plumbing, and Control UI navigation/rendering.
- Security follow-up: Plugin UI entry registration, launch/session auth, and iframe bridge proxying now validate canonical decoded plugin roots, so encoded dot-segment paths cannot let one plugin UI session authorize sibling plugin routes.
- Scope boundary: This PR does not add the Session Search plugin or any plugin-specific UI. The proof plugin lives separately on [`Ittiz/openclaw@forge/session-search-plugin`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/tree/forge/session-search-plugin).

## What Problem This Solves

Plugins can expose gateway-authenticated HTTP routes, but before this PR they had no generic, first-class way to place a Control UI navigation entry that launches a plugin-owned UI through the gateway authorization boundary. This PR adds that contract while keeping plugin entry paths and iframe bridge requests confined to the owning plugin root.

## Working Example Plugin

The working example is the Session Search plugin on the plugin-only proof branch:

- Branch: [`Ittiz/openclaw@forge/session-search-plugin`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/tree/forge/session-search-plugin)
- README: [`README.md`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/blob/forge/session-search-plugin/README.md)
- Entry point registration: [`index.ts`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/blob/forge/session-search-plugin/index.ts)
- Manifest: [`openclaw.plugin.json`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/blob/forge/session-search-plugin/openclaw.plugin.json)

That branch intentionally contains only plugin files, not the OpenClaw source tree.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof

- Current head tested: `b8e35876a18a36390c867080e475d53f26fb4e15`.
- Behavior addressed: Plugin UI entry points can be listed/launched/rendered; sandboxed opaque-origin iframes receive the MessagePort bridge; decoded/canonicalized plugin paths remain confined to the owning plugin root; new entry-point response schemas are exported through the public protocol barrel.
- Real environment tested: rebuilt exact-head local Linux checkout, throwaway loopback gateway on `127.0.0.1:19888`, temporary plugin loaded through `plugins.load.paths`, Control UI driven in a real headless Chromium browser, plus direct HTTP route checks.

Commands run after the current fix:

```bash
git diff --check
node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/navigation.browser.test.ts --reporter=verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-host-hooks.test.ts src/gateway/server.plugin-http-auth.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts ui/src/ui/navigation.test.ts --reporter=verbose
OPENCLAW_BUILD_ALL_NO_PNPM=1 node scripts/build-all.mjs ciArtifacts
node .artifacts/pr80388-live-proof/run-proof.mjs
```

Live browser/plugin-route evidence from the throwaway exact-head gateway:

```json
{
  "head": "OpenClaw 2026.6.8 (726f348)",
  "controlUiUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:19888/overview#token=REDACTED",
  "pluginEntryVisible": true,
  "iframeMarker": "PR80388_PROOF_PLUGIN_IFRAME_READY",
  "bridgeText": "BRIDGE_STATUS: 200 {\"ok\":true,\"marker\":\"PR80388_PROOF_PLUGIN_API_READY\",\"pluginId\":\"proof-entry\"}",
  "directUnauthStatus": 401,
  "directAuthStatus": 200,
  "directAuthBody": "{\"ok\":true,\"marker\":\"PR80388_PROOF_PLUGIN_API_READY\",\"pluginId\":\"proof-entry\"}"
}
```

Focused test evidence:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/navigation.browser.test.ts --reporter=verbose
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  17 passed (17)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 9.24s

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-host-hooks.test.ts src/gateway/server.plugin-http-auth.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts ui/src/ui/navigation.test.ts --reporter=verbose
Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests  68 passed (68)
[test] passed 3 Vitest shards in 68.19s

$ node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check
package-exported forbidden subpaths: 0

$ OPENCLAW_BUILD_ALL_NO_PNPM=1 node scripts/build-all.mjs ciArtifacts
[build-all] phase timings: total 175.6s
```

Specific regression locked in:

- `ui/src/ui/navigation.browser.test.ts` verifies sandboxed in-app plugin requests are proxied only for plugin-owned paths and stale iframe bridge ports cannot service current-frame requests.
- `src/gateway/server.plugin-http-auth.test.ts` rejects Plugin UI Entry Point sessions when decoded path candidates leave the plugin root.
- `src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts` rejects encoded dot-segment sibling escapes at entry-point registration.
- `ui/src/ui/navigation.test.ts` rejects external, contextual, cross-plugin, and encoded sibling paths.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes. Plugins can expose Control UI entry points.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Yes. Entry-point launch uses short-lived Plugin UI launch/session tokens.
- Data access scope changed? Yes. Entry points declare required operator scopes.
- Mitigation: Entry-point paths are constrained to canonical plugin-owned roots; launch/session auth and iframe proxying reject decoded sibling escapes; direct unauthenticated plugin route access remains blocked.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.

target.title: acp: cancel queued SessionActorQueue items on caller abort
target.body:
This PR is narrower than #52747 and targets a different failure mode:
it makes already-enqueued `SessionActorQueue` items cancellable on
caller abort, so `pendingBySession` does not stay inflated when the
predecessor tail never settles. Complementary to #52747's lane task
timeout work and #80239's Pi abort cleanup; does not add any config
or runtime timeout behavior.

## Summary

When a caller's `AbortSignal` fires before its actor reaches the head
of the per-key queue:

- the queued slot is marked cancelled (per-call closure flag),
- `pendingBySession` is decremented eagerly (idempotent, exactly once),
- the wrapped task throws `AbortError` instead of running `op()` when
  its turn arrives,
- per-key serialization invariant is preserved (later same-key tasks
  still wait for predecessor).

`withSessionActor` now forwards its existing signal to
`actorQueue.run(actorKey, op, { signal })` and the manager-side
abort-handling is simplified. The existing diagnostic log line
(`acp-manager: actor queue abort-before-start ...`) is preserved.

## Why

Without caller-side cancellation, `withSessionActor` could reject the
caller's promise on abort-before-start but had no way to remove or
cancel the already-enqueued item. If the predecessor tail did not
settle, the queued item could not reach `finally(onSettle)`, so
`pendingBySession` could not decrement and liveness diagnostics
reported `queued=N last=run:completed` for the actor key
indefinitely.

Sandbox repro reproduces the stale state in a few lines using a
non-settling first task, a same-key enqueue, an abort-before-start
on the second caller, and a measurement of `pendingBySession`.

## What changed

- `src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.ts`: `run()` accepts an
  optional `signal: AbortSignal`. Per-call closure tracks `cancelled`,
  `actorStarted`, `pendingDecremented` flags. Signal listener
  decrements pending eagerly when actor has not started; the wrapped
  task throws `AbortError` when the cancelled slot reaches the queue
  head. Public Plugin SDK (`src/plugin-sdk/keyed-async-queue.ts`) is
  not changed — cancellation is implemented one level up.
- `src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts`: `withSessionActor` passes
  its existing signal to `actorQueue.run(actorKey, op, { signal })`.
  Manager-side `markAbortBeforeStart` call and the inline abort
  listener are no longer needed on this path; the queue takes over.
  Diagnostic log preserved verbatim.
- `src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.test.ts`: 7 new tests
  covering signal-based cancellation, eager pending decrement,
  idempotent decrement, per-key serialization preservation,
  pre-aborted signal, queue still alive after cancelled item,
  backwards-compatibility for callers without signal. Existing
  diagnostic test preserved.

## Verification

pnpm test src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.test.ts
✓ 8 passed

pnpm test src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.test.ts
          src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts
✓ 64 passed

pnpm check:changed       ✓ EXIT 0
pnpm build               ✓ EXIT 0
pnpm exec oxfmt --check  ✓ clean

Live runtime validation on macOS gateway:
- Built from cherry-pick onto `v2026.5.6` tag
- Restarted gateway with patched `dist/` (smoke run 4.6s, status ok)
- Patched bundle contains `Aborted before start`, `pendingDecremented`,
  `actor queue abort-before-start` markers — fix loaded in memory
- 24h passive monitor observed no new
  `queued=N last=run:completed` stale liveness warnings

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Caller abort-before-start for already-enqueued `SessionActorQueue` items no longer leaves `pendingBySession` inflated when the predecessor tail never settles.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS live OpenClaw gateway setup, patched npm install built from cherry-pick onto `v2026.5.6`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Cherry-picked the source fix onto `v2026.5.6`, built, rsynced patched `dist/` into the live npm install, restarted the gateway, then ran a live smoke request against the restarted gateway process.
- **Evidence after fix:** Live output/runtime evidence from the patched setup: gateway restarted with pid `18313`; smoke run completed in `4.6s` with `status ok`; no Codex schema errors were observed. Patched bundle `dist/manager-D7F0BwP2.js` contains the after-fix markers `Aborted before start`, `pendingDecremented`, and `actor queue abort-before-start`. A 24h passive monitor is still running and has observed `0` stale queue warnings so far.
- **Observed result after fix:** The patched live gateway stayed up after restart, the smoke request succeeded, the patched queue cancellation markers were loaded in the running bundle, and passive monitoring has not seen new `queued=N last=run:completed` stale liveness warnings.
- **What was not tested:** Cross-OS live gateway validation was not repeated for this PR; local validation covered tests, changed checks, build, and formatter.

## Notes for reviewers

- This is intentionally separate from #52747 because the failure
  modes differ: #52747 targets a lane task that has *started but is
  stuck*, while this PR targets a caller cancellation *before the
  actor starts*. The two are complementary; either can be merged
  without conflicting with the other's intent, though both touch
  `session-actor-queue.ts` and `manager.core.ts` and will require a
  rebase of the second-merged PR.
- Public Plugin SDK is intentionally untouched. Backwards
  compatibility: `run(actorKey, op)` without `options` behaves
  identically to before.
- An earlier deployment experiment confirmed that `dist`-only
  patching across version lines is unsafe (Codex schema + Control
  UI mismatch). The fix in this PR is targeted at the source level
  and rebuilds cleanly on the same release line.
- May address parts of #13744 (session lock timeout) by reducing
  queue pressure that contributes to lock contention, though
  #13744's primary scope is the lock itself.

target.title: fix: clear cross-run messaging-tool sent-text state after every compaction
target.body:
## Problem

When a run completes and the session compacts, the arrays tracking texts sent via messaging tools (`message(action=send)`, `sessions_send`, etc.) were only cleared on compaction retries (`willRetry=true`). On normal compaction (`willRetry=false`), stale entries survived into the next run.

`isMessagingToolDuplicateNormalized()` compares the final reply text against these stale entries using substring matching (`normalized.includes(normalizedSent)`). If the new run's text happens to share a normalized substring with any entry from the previous run, the block reply is silently dropped with:

> Skipping message_end block reply - already sent via messaging tool

## Fix

Clear `messagingToolSentTexts`, `messagingToolSentTextsNormalized`, `messagingToolSentTargets`, and `messagingToolSentMediaUrls` unconditionally in the `compaction_end` handler — before the `willRetry` branch — so the next run always starts with a clean slate.

## Verification

- Relevant tests pass (6/6) in `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.compaction.test.ts` (updated fixture)
- No logic change to `emitBlockChunk`, `handleMessageEnd`, or the dedup function itself — change is purely about state lifecycle between runs

target.title: feat(cron): include event time in failure alerts
target.body:
## Summary

Closes #77497.

Cron failure notifications now include the failed event time instead of relying only on the delivery timestamp. The timestamp uses the cron schedule timezone when present and falls back to UTC for non-cron schedules or missing timezones.

## What changed

- Added a shared cron alert timestamp formatter.
- Included event time in single failure destination notifications.
- Included the last event time in repeated failure and skipped-run alerts.
- Included event time in schedule-error auto-disable notifications.
- Plumbed the event timestamp through failure alert delivery params and webhook payloads.

## User impact

Delayed cron alerts are less misleading after gateway restarts or host connectivity outages because the alert body names when the cron failure happened.

## Validation

- `CI=true OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts src/cron/service/jobs.schedule-error-isolation.test.ts`
- `CI=true OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cron/alert-time.ts src/gateway/server-cron-notifications.ts src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/state.ts src/gateway/server-cron.ts src/cron/service/jobs.ts src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts src/cron/service/jobs.schedule-error-isolation.test.ts`
- `CI=true OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- exec tsgo -b tsconfig.projects.json`
- `CI=true OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- run check:changed`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Cron failure alert text now includes the cron event timestamp so delayed channel delivery does not look like a fresh failure.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout in `/private/tmp/openclaw-cron-alert-timestamps` on macOS, Node v25.8.1, pnpm 10.33.2.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran the cron alert formatter directly from the local checkout and ran the focused cron/gateway validation commands listed above.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the patched formatter:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e "import { formatCronAlertEventTime } from './src/cron/alert-time.ts'; console.log(formatCronAlertEventTime({ job: { schedule: { kind: 'cron', expr: '0 11 * * *', tz: 'Asia/Dubai' } }, eventTimeMs: Date.parse('2026-05-04T07:20:00.000Z') })); console.log(formatCronAlertEventTime({ job: { schedule: { kind: 'every', everyMs: 60000 } }, eventTimeMs: Date.parse('2026-05-04T07:20:00.000Z') }));"
2026-05-04 11:20 (Asia/Dubai)
2026-05-04 07:20 (UTC)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The formatter emits a human-readable event time in the scheduled timezone and falls back to UTC when no cron timezone exists; the focused gateway/cron tests verify those timestamps appear in failure alert bodies.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run a live Telegram outage/reconnect scenario; validation covered local gateway notification paths and cron service alert generation.

target.title: [codex] Further optimize Telegram media and reply context flow
target.body:
## Summary

- add scoped indexes to the Telegram message cache so `recentBefore` and `around` avoid scanning and sorting the full cache
- cache successful Telegram media resolutions by file identity and relevant runtime options
- revalidate cached media paths before reuse so TTL cleanup cannot leave stale attachments behind
- add regression coverage for thread-scoped chat windows, media cache hits, and stale media cache eviction
- bump `@openclaw/fs-safe` to 0.2.1 so focused Telegram tests can install and resolve `@openclaw/fs-safe/config` cleanly

## Context

This builds on earlier Telegram-route performance work by `obviyus`, further optimizing the same flow by tightening reply-context lookups and avoiding repeated Telegram media hydration.

## Why

Telegram reply-context building can repeatedly query recent chat windows and hydrate the same reply media. This PR keeps those hot paths bounded while preserving the existing chat-wide and thread-specific behavior.

The media cache also checks the saved file still exists before returning a cached path, because long-running gateways can remove saved media through TTL cleanup.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram reply-context lookups stay thread-scoped or chat-wide as requested, repeated Telegram media hydration reuses the cached saved path, and a deleted saved media file is evicted and re-downloaded instead of returning a stale path.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway from this PR head (`b8ad83191132ea2cdb1c9a40312ded58f58444ba`) on macOS, running `OpenClaw 2026.5.10-beta.1` with the Telegram plugin loaded from the PR checkout. Telegram ingress used the real Bot API webhook for `@acecoderbot` through a temporary Cloudflare tunnel. The LLM provider was mocked only to make the response deterministic; Telegram update delivery, reply-context hydration, message-cache persistence, and reply dispatch all ran through the PR checkout.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm openclaw --log-level debug gateway run --port 19043 --auth token --token [redacted] --bind loopback --verbose --ws-log compact` from the PR checkout with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, a real Telegram Bot API webhook, and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible provider. Sent `PR80243 live seed OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_1` to `@acecoderbot`, then sent a real Telegram reply to that DM with `PR80243 live reply-context OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_2`. Also ran a local media/cache proof through `node --import tsx` using a real temporary state directory and local trusted Bot API file path.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live runtime logs and persisted cache/model-input excerpts from the actual PR gateway run:


target.title: feat(discord): add implicit reply mention policy
target.body:
Closes #80234.

Adds `channels.discord.implicitReplyMentions`:

```json5
{
  fromUsers: true,
  fromBots: true
}
```

Defaults preserve current behavior. Multi-bot deployments can set `fromBots: false` so bot-authored native replies do not wake another bot via the implicit `reply_to_bot` path; explicit `<@botId>` mentions still work.

Checks run:

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Discord native reply-only mentions can wake an OpenClaw bot when another bot replies to one of its messages. This PR adds a policy knob so deployments can keep human reply activation while disabling bot-authored reply-only activation.

Real environment tested: Private Discord server with a private test channel and two OpenClaw Discord bot accounts. Server, channel, bot names, message IDs, and account IDs are intentionally omitted from this public proof.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. In the private test channel, send an anchor message from the target bot.
2. Send a native Discord reply from a separate relay bot to that anchor message. The reply body contains no textual `<@bot>` mention.
3. Observe whether the target bot wakes/responds.
4. Repeat after applying this branch with `implicitReplyMentions: { fromUsers: true, fromBots: false }`.
5. Send a separate control message from the relay bot that contains a real textual `<@bot>` mention and asks the target bot to reply with a known marker.

Evidence after fix: Redacted live output excerpt from the private Discord test channel:

```text
after-fix native reply-only test:
relay bot | "oc-proof-80235-after4-231018 native-reply-no-explicit-mention" | ref=<target-bot-anchor>
UNEXPECTED_TARGET_RESPONSES=0

explicit mention control:
target bot | "oc-proof-80235-control-231107 explicit-mention-ok" | ref=<relay-bot-control-message>
EXPLICIT_CONTROL_OK=true
```

Observed result after fix: With `fromBots:false`, the bot-authored native reply-only message did not wake the target bot during the observation window. A real textual explicit mention still woke the target bot and produced the requested marker response.

What was not tested: Human-authored reply-only behavior was covered by unit tests but not repeated in the live Discord proof. PluralKit behavior was covered by unit tests but not repeated in the live Discord proof.

target.title: [codex] Add Codex continuity bridge
target.body:
## Summary

Adds the Codex Continuity Bridge to the bundled Codex plugin.

The bridge keeps Codex UI as the high-bandwidth workbench while giving OpenClaw a quiet operator surface for Telegram: status, recent threads, explicit watched completion checks, evidence-separated handoff briefs, and a gated write path that is disabled by default.

## Safety Model

- Read-only Codex app-server status is preferred.
- SQLite fallback is read-only and explicitly stale-labeled.
- Raw Codex app-server blobs are not exposed by the public HTTP routes.
- Watch notifications are explicit, deduped, redacted, and quiet by default.
- Telegram-to-Codex writes require an explicit feature flag plus provenance, trusted sender checks, repo allowlisting, risk gating, unambiguous target selection, and confirmed app-server write methods.
- SQLite is never used as a write path.

## Validation

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/codex/tsconfig.json --noEmit`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/codex/src/continuity/bridge.test.ts extensions/codex/src/commands.test.ts` (89 tests)
- Local gateway smoke test for `/codex/status`, `/codex/threads`, `/codex/watch`, `/codex/watch/check`, `/codex/handoff`, and `/codex/goal`
